Yes! There are a couple of places you can go to discuss PyKMIP. We have an IRC channel, #pykmip, on Freenode, hosted at https://webchat.freenode.net/. Login is free and registration is optional, if you haven't tried it before. We try to hang out there on the channel on a regular basis.

You can also send me or @rellerreller email with any questions you may have. This project isn't too active, and there aren't many people working on it, so we don't have a dedicated mailing list for it yet.

As for your questions, the `KmipSession` is a thread object that will be spun off from a new `KmipServer` class that I am finishing up right now; it's all part of the main KMIP server rewrite I've been working on for the past few months. The `KmipServer` handles receiving client connections and spins off `KmipSessions` for each one. The session then parses the incoming request and (if parse successful) passes the resulting KMIP request object to the `KmipEngine` for processing.

Since the `KmipServer` isn't out yet, there's no direct way to invoke `KmipSessions` yet without creating your own framework for it, which it sounds like you're doing with the SocketServer/AsynchronousMixins. It is definitely possible to run a `KmipSession` directly as your main thread; instead of invoking `KmipSession.start` (which kicks off the separate threaded-ness of the session) just call `KmipSession.run` directly.

The reason I wanted to avoid using the current thread's name is because the intent is to have multiple `KmipSessions` at once and I wanted to avoid any thread naming collisions. While I don't think there's anything technically incorrect about that thread naming scheme, thread activity is logged by thread name and I wanted to make it easy for those looking through the logs to distinguish between different threads.

Does that answer your questions? Definitely let me know if you have more. I'm happy to help.

I see, so normalization for 'ce' is with respect to the number of instances (the size of the batch dimension), never w.r.t to the number of valid tokens in the batch (i.e. the number of non-pad tokens in labels). This is consistent to what SoftmaxOutput backpropagates if args.normalize_loss is off (i.e. batch normalization of loss, using rescale_grad=1/batch_size).

If args.normalize_loss is on, SoftmaxOutput from the CrossEntropyLoss is using 'valid' normalization, effectively normalizing by the number of valid label tokens in the batch.

For these Eve metrics, we could use batch normalization as default ( `num_inst += label.shape[0]`) if args.normalize_loss is off, and `num_inst += mx.nd.sum(labels != C.PAD_ID)` if args.normalize_loss is on. 
However, doing that consistent for both CE and SCE metrics would also require overwriting the CE metric. Not sure if we want to do this... In any case, it might be hard to exactly get the same loss from the metric as is used to calculate gradients in the graph.

Use of the environment variable named `PIP_USER` with `python setup.py install` is confusing since `pip` is not being used here AND `$PIP_USER` is meaningful for the `pip` command.

For example, if this line were `pip install .`, the value of `PIP_USER` can be anything that resolves to true. e.g. `PIP_USER=1 pip install .` is equivalent to `pip install --user`.

My recommendation is to either install with `pip install .` for both platforms, specifying `PIP_USER=1` only in linux (or wherever else required) OR leave it as-is, renaming `PIP_USER` to something like `USER_OPTIONS` with the value `--user` (and any additional options specifically required by the environment).

Note: prior to the NEB commits, half_kpoint_first was hardcoded to False for the other custodian types. I am pretty sure that at some time, this parameter was defaulted to True in custodian (which I did not like since I am a big fan of principle of least surprise and "do no harm" for default parameters) and so in atomate I defaulted it to False so there were no surprises of a user's kpoint mesh changing. Since then, custodian now has half_kpoint_first = False as the default and there is no reason to enforce this in atomate. It is for the same reason that atomate sets auto_npar to False since for >1 node jobs this parameter can lead to unintended and confusing behavior when the user is trying to set NPAR themselves (as we saw from some user reports) yet never explicitly told custodian to change their NPAR.

For the moment, @HanmeiTang should make two changes. (i) document all new parameters as optional parameters and (ii) make sure the new optional parameters get used for all job types not just NEB (with the same defaults as before).

In the future, I will probably try to clean up some of this with a more **kwargs style approach so there isn't a need to add a new parameter to atomate every time there is a new custodian option. The only thing atomate may want to do is to deflect some of the default behavior of custodian to be more in line with atomate's principles for how workflows should operate by default.

I did some reading and my understanding is that `add_periodic_task()` (http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/periodic-tasks.html#entries) can be used to do this, however it needs to have a `@app.on_after_configure.connect` decorator in order to be processed at any point after initial configuration has been complete. Since `add_periodic_task()` sets `celery.conf.beat_schedule` in the background, we would also need to update `celery.conf.beat_schedule` under the `@app.on_after_configure.connect` decorator. However, this decorator seems to have no effect when used here in `health.py`. I suspect this is because the extensions get initialized too late, after the `on_after_configure` event has already fired and been missed.

Additionally, there seemed to be some stackoverflow comments on this here:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/41124591/setting-up-periodic-tasks-in-celery-celerybeat-dynamically-using-add-periodic


> In order to be able to dynamically manage periodic tasks and reschedule celerybeat at runtime, there’s the django-celery-beat extension that stores the schedule in the Django database, and presents a convenient admin interface to manage periodic tasks at runtime.

Which sounds to me like dynamic celery beat schedule updating only works with the django extension.

> One question I had is about the viability of using the lexicon project you found for many of our DNS plugins including this one. All of the DNS plugins that have been implemented so far are supposedly supported by lexicon with the exception of Google Cloud DNS which they claim to be open to accepting a PR for. If we go this route, I'd also be curious if restructuring our DNS plugins makes sense if there's a common base and level of abstraction from lexicon we can use to easily support all/many of these DNS providers.

In many cases, Lexicon does not provide the functionality we need. Namely: it does not provide functionality for determining the DNS zone which should be modified.

As background: DNS APIs expose the concept of a DNS zone in various ways. Essentially, a DNS zone is a subtree of the overall DNS record tree. The DNS zone for `_acme-challenge.foo.bar.example.com` may be rooted at `_acme-challenge.foo.bar.example.com`, `foo.bar.example.com`, `bar.example.com`, or `example.com` depending on way the provider and the way the user is using the provider. For all DNS APIs I've encountered so far, DNS record creation requests are scoped to a DNS zone. As a result, it is necessary to determine the zone id (called a domain id by some providers).

Most providers have a query-like process for determining the zone id, but that is not exposed by Lexicon.

In some cases, like CloudXNS (see #4522), guess-and-check is the best we can do with or with or without Lexicon. For those, I think using Lexicon makes sense.

For providers that offer a clear query API to determine the zone id, I don't think we should attempt to determine it though guess-and-check (which means not using Lexicon for those providers).

There are also other reasons not to use Lexicon when a better alternative is available:

1. Lexicon often suppresses details when something goes wrong. With `cloudflare`, we can provide distinct error messages for the following cases: invalid credentials, malformed credentials, the domain not being associated with the Cloudflare account, and other errors. With `lexicon`, we would not be able to distinguish those cases.
2. With few exceptions, Lexicon does not use existing API libraries. Using a provider's official library makes it much easier to go to them and ask for help if something breaks. 
3. Lexicon provides a minimum subset of functionality. This is convenient for providing a consistent CLI for managing DNS records. This is less convenient for our use cases; some providers' APIs include functionality to determine whether a record update has been saved or whether a record update has finished propagating (from the provider's point of view). We don't have access to that functionality through Lexicon.

> We split it out into its own package.

If we address #1279, I think this would be worth considering regardless of the dependency discussion; it seems like few users will ever use _all_ DNS plugins. (That said, I think there are arguments to be made in favor of a simple monolithic distribution model that provides a consistent experience for all users.)

> We vendorize the dependencies that aren't widely packaged by OSes such as `cloudflare` and `logger`. I haven't looked into exactly what we'd need to vendorize here but I'm happy to do so if we go this route.

`logging` is just a standard python library, but yes: we could vendorize `cloudflare`.

I don't know much about python dependency management, but after reading a [random article](http://bitprophet.org/blog/2012/06/07/on-vendorizing/), this seems like the right call.

I spent a bit of time on sorting out the OAuth flow today. The registration is best done via the UI and is a bit more involved. It requires the following config change
1. Modify webserver_config.py
```
AUTH_TYPE = AUTH_OAUTH
OAUTH_PROVIDERS = [
{
    'name':'google', 
    'whitelist': ['@COMPANY.COM'],
    'token_key':'access_token',
    'icon':'fa-google',
        'remote_app': {
            'base_url':'https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v2/',
            'request_token_params':{
                'scope': 'email profile'
            },
            'access_token_url':'https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/token',
            'authorize_url':'https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth',
            'request_token_url': None,
            'consumer_key': CONSUMER_KEY,
            'consumer_secret': SECRET_KEY,
        }
    }
]
AUTH_USER_REGISTRATION = True
AUTH_USER_REGISTRATION_ROLE = "Admin"
```
This will allow the admin to self register an account with full access
2. Once the admin(s) is registered, modify webserver_config.py again:
```
AUTH_USER_REGISTRATION_ROLE = "Public"
```
This will allow all other users to freely register with the `Public` role via OAuth.
